text: Call me Ishmael. Some years ago — never mind how long precisely — having little or no money in my purse, and nothing particular to interest me on shore, I thought I would sail about a little and see the watery part of the world. It is a way I have of driving off the spleen and regulating the circulation. I have often stood at the crossroads in life, beset by the dreariness of existence and the desperation that comes with feeling trapped. The morbid fascinations that I find myself drawn to - visiting funeral processions, imagining a chaotic world - seem an unwholesome substitute for a respite from my melancholy. Yet, as I stand there, weighed down by the thoughts that plague me, I begin to crave the freedom of the open sea, a haven from which I can temporarily escape the turmoil within. This is my substitute for pistol and ball. With a philosophical flourish Cato throws himself upon his sword; I quietly take to the ship.

Alice was beginning to get very tired of sitting by her sister on the bank, and of having nothing to do. Once or twice she had peeped into the book her sister was reading, but it had no pictures or conversations in it. And what is the use of a book, thought Alice, without pictures or conversations? As she sat there, lost in thought about the meaninglessness of a pictureless book, a small, delicate figure caught her eye - a White Rabbit with pink eyes, dressed in a waistcoat, carrying a pocket watch and looking rather flustered as it hurried by, muttering "Oh dear! Oh dear!" to itself. Its usual calm demeanor was replaced by an air of urgency, causing Alice's curiosity to get the better of her. She watched intently as the rabbit paused for just a moment, glanced around nervously, and then vanished into thin air. But when the Rabbit actually took a watch out of its waistcoat-pocket, and looked at it, and then hurried on, Alice started to her feet, for it flashed across her mind that she had never before seen a rabbit with either a waistcoat-pocket, or a watch to take out of it. Burning with curiosity, she ran across the field after it, and was just in time to see it pop down a large rabbit-hole under the hedge.

She knew this about him, this ability to dismiss anything remotely emotional as beneath his refined standards. Yet, she had managed to penetrate that façade, just once. He never spoke of the softer passions, save with a gibe and a sneer. They were admirable things for the observer, excellent for drawing the veil from men's motives and actions. But for the trained reasoner to admit such intrusions into his own delicate and finely adjusted temperament was to introduce a distracting factor which might throw a doubt upon all his mental results.

This passage is setting the tone for the rest of the book by highlighting the paradoxical nature of life in 18th century France. The text describes a time of great upheaval and uncertainty, where different people had conflicting opinions about the future and what was to come.

The use of parallel structures (e.g. "it was the best of times... it was the worst of times") creates a sense of rhythm and emphasizes the dual nature of the period being described. The contrast between light and darkness, hope and despair also adds to this sense of complexity and paradox.

Dickens' passage is also notable for its use of hyperbole ("we had nothing before us", "in short, the period was so far like the present period") which creates a sense of exaggeration and emphasizes the uncertainty and chaos of the time. The final sentence, where Dickens likens the past to last year's clothes, suggests that the past is being dismissed as old-fashioned or irrelevant.

Overall, this passage sets the tone for the rest of the book by establishing the themes of duality, paradox, and the complexity of human experience in the face of uncertainty and change.

"My dear Mr. Bennet," said his lady to him one day, "have you heard that Netherfield Park is let at last?"

Mr. Bennet replied, "I have not."  Then replied Mrs. Bennet, "But it is--and so the ladies of Longbourn are in a tower of despair; for if Mr. Bingley does not marry his niece, there will be no doubt that the long vacant seat at Netherfield will be let to some collateral branch of the family."

Mr. Bennet added, "But I think I see a very pretty quizzical expression on the face of Mrs. Bennet as she walked into the room."

This novel is written from the perspective of a narrator who provides explanations and insights throughout the story, often taking an omniscient view of the characters' thoughts and feelings.

The novel begins with an introduction to the Bennet family: Mr. Bennet and his wife, Mrs. Bennet, along with their five daughters: Jane, Elizabeth, Mary, Kitty, and Lydia. The Bennets are a lower-middle-class family living in Longbourn, a modest estate on the outskirts of Meryton.

The first plot point is introduced when Mrs. Bennet reveals that Netherfield Park has been rented by Mr. Bingley, a wealthy young bachelor who lives nearby. She expresses her concerns about her daughters' futures and her hope that they will be married off to secure their financial stability. The arrival of Mr. Bingley sets the stage for the novel's exploration of love, class, social status, and the limited options available to women in 19th-century England.

The tone of the novel is witty and satirical, with a focus on the social conventions and hypocrisies of the time. The narrator provides commentary on the characters' actions and motivations, often highlighting their flaws and weaknesses. The novel's use of humor, irony, and social satire creates a lively and engaging reading experience.

Overall, Pride and Prejudice is a classic romance that explores themes of love, family, and social class in 19th-century England. Its witty dialogue, engaging characters, and insightful commentary on society make it a beloved and enduring work of literature.

"My dear Mr. Bennet," said his lady to him one day, "have you heard that Netherfield Park is let at last?"

Mr. Bennet replied that he had not.

"How soon can it be?" she added; "for I am determined to visit him as soon as he can have them in the house; and since woman is naturally covetous, I dare say I shall be very pleasant company."

Mr. Bennet was glad to hear this, because he trusted that the visit would be some time or other more than usually welcome.

His lady was counting out her money with an intent look, as she listened to the first half of his answer; and when she had finished it, she said no more till he had told them what the second night before had brought home.  Mr. Bennet therefore set out for Netherfield in company with his five daughters.

They arrived there in the afternoon of the same day. As they drove up to the house, a man came flying down on horseback and stopped before them all.  "Hold! hold! pray tell, my dear young ladies, what has caused this sudden agitation?" he cried.  A gentleman of large figure, with a straggling beard, joined in it; "I have just come from London—"

"...and I bring the most important intelligence," interrupted an elderly lady on horseback.  The gentlemen all turned to her as she came up, and began to listen with an eager air.

Mr. Bennet bowed, and sat down without answering a word; the ladies, however, looked at each other in dismay--"She is a great deal too ill to be moved. What is your meaning by sending for us?"  "We have been informed that Mr. Bingley, in consequence of information received through a third party, will be near them in less than a week."

Mr. Bennet was glad to hear this; his daughters were equally so.

On the following morning Elizabeth Bennet had her eyes on him as he drove by the house.  She watched him till he disappeared from view.

"I wish I could see him," said she, "but I know it is impossible."

As the ladies were sitting in the drawling room some time after dinner, Elizabeth Bennet could not help thinking of Netherfield and its master, as well as of Mr. Darcy's character; and her thoughts were carried away by the pleasant remembrance of his mannered reserve, which she had been assured was most disagreeable.

"Mr. Bingley," said one of the younger girls, "is certainly very agreeable in manner."

He has neither meddled with my affairs nor insulted me."  The gentleman laughed and said: "I will never speak to him again if he marries Miss Bennet."

"I would not be so fastidious as you are," she rejoined; "for a man of large fortune, like Mr. Bingley, must have some qualities to make his marriage desirable."

The younger girls all looked at each as if wondering if the elder was telling her half the story, and she hastily added:  "Why, my dear Mr. Bennet, are you so gloomy?"

"Because I know that Mr. Bingley does not love me," said Elizabeth.

"You shall hear of him again," said Mr. Bennet; "and when you do, there is time enough for what shall then be the subject of that visit."

In a few days Mr. Bingley returned from his visit to Netherfield and was soon announced at the assembly rooms, where he was eagerly seen by all the young ladies in the neighbourhood.

"I am not at all surprised," said Elizabeth, as they walked into the assembly room together with their parents.

"You must go in and look for a man you know," her sister Lydia whispered.

Elizabeth Bennet could not help feeling that she had been too much deceived.  Her first impression was highly favourable, but it was speedily superseded by a deep consciousness of inferiority.  "What did he say to you now?" Mr. Collins asked his mother.

"You must make hasty connections," said the lady; "he will be in Netherfield in six weeks, and I will know him as soon as he arrives."

"I am going out to-morrow."

"Let us have some breakfast on the lawn," said Mr. Bennet.

It is a way I have of driving off the spleen and regulating the circulation.  Whenever I find myself growing grim about the mouth; whenever it is a damp, drizzly November in my soul;  whenever I find myself involuntarily pausing before coffin warehouses, and bringing up the rear of every funeral I meet; and especially whenever my hypos get such an upper hand of me, that it requires a strong moral principle to prevent me from deliberately stepping into the street, and methodically knocking people's hats off — then I account it high time to get to sea as soon as I can.  This is my substitute for pistol practice; with a Hudson River whaler I have learned to look sharp on the main-mast scale of life and death, and we have had some hard luck; but so far, she has held her way well.

Nowhere does one man know that he might be within an ace of being a mighty hunter, or that the gleam in another man's eye, which he would as soon not see, means nothing more nor less than “I will cut your throat from ear to ear.”  In this world a man has got to pick and choose.  The men who really get ahead are those who pick wisely.

The men who really get ahead are those who know how to use their environment.  I have no special talent for fretting over my physical condition, but the sea air and exercise seem to do me good; and besides, it is a great comfort to be able to say that I am doing what I love — and that is precisely what I am doing.

But if you ever find yourself on the deck of a whaling ship in an Englishman’s or American’s arm, remember this:  The crew may be in a good temper, but they can turn on you as quick as a flash.  There are no such things as perfect men; only perfection is found in one another, and the fact that there have been no revolutions since the days of Caesar has to do with how much better we must all think ourselves than other people.

This is the opening passage of Herman Melville's novel "Moby-Dick". It introduces Ishmael, the narrator, and his motivations for sailing on the whaling ship Pequod. The passage sets the tone for the rest of the book, which explores themes of obsession, the human condition, and the complexities of human nature.

The passage is notable for its poetic style, which creates a sense of atmosphere and mood. Melville uses imagery and metaphor to describe Ishmael's emotions and thoughts, as well as his physical surroundings. For example, he writes, "A damp, drizzly November in my soul," which creates a vivid picture of Ishmael's inner state.

The passage also introduces several themes that are central to the novel, including the idea that men must pick and choose in life, and that perfection is not found in individuals, but rather in relationships. These themes are developed throughout the book, as Ishmael navigates his relationships with the other characters on the Pequod.

Overall, the passage is a masterpiece of literary style and world-building, and it sets the stage for the rest of the novel to explore the complexities of human nature and the search for meaning in life.

It was the best of times, it was the worst of times, it was the age of wisdom, it was the age of foolishness, it was the epoch of belief, it was the epoch of incredulity, it was the season of Light, it was the season of Darkness, it was the spring of hope, it was the winter of despair. We had everything before us, we had nothing before us, we were all going direct to Heaven, we were all going direct the other way. In short, the period was so far like the present period, that some of its noisiest authorities insisted on its being received, for good or for evil, in the superlative degree of comparison only. There were a king with a large jaw and a queen with a plain face, on the throne of England; there were a king with a large jaw and a queen with a fair face, on the throne of France. In both countries it was clearer than crystal to the lords of the State preserves of loaves and fishes, that things in general were settled for ever.

Alice was beginning to get very tired of sitting by her sister on the bank, and of having nothing to do. Once or twice she had peeped into the book her sister was reading, but it had no pictures or conversations in it. And what is the use of a book, thought Alice, without pictures or conversations? So she was considering in her own mind, whether the pleasure of making a daisy-chain would be worth the trouble of getting up and picking the daisies, when suddenly a White Rabbit with pink eyes ran close by her. There was nothing so very remarkable in that; nor did Alice think it so very much out of the way to hear the Rabbit say to itself, Oh dear! Oh dear! I shall be late! But when the Rabbit actually took a watch out of its waistcoat-pocket, and looked at it, and then hurried on, Alice started to her feet, for it flashed across her mind that she had never before seen a rabbit with either a waistcoat-pocket, or a watch to take out of it. Burning with curiosity, she ran across the field after it, and was just in time to see it pop down a large rabbit-hole under the hedge.

It is a truth universally acknowledged, that a single man in possession of a good fortune, must be in want of a wife. However little known the feelings or views of such a man may be on his first entering a neighbourhood, this truth is so well fixed in the minds of the surrounding families, that he is considered the rightful property of some one or other of their daughters. My dear Mr. Bennet, said his lady to him one day, have you heard that Netherfield Park is let at last? Mr. Bennet replied that he had not. But it is, returned she; for Mrs. Long has just been here, and she told me all about it. Mr. Bennet made no answer. Do not you want to know who has taken it? cried his wife impatiently. You want to tell me, and I have no objection to hearing it. This was invitation enough. Why, my dear, you must know, Mrs. Long says that Netherfield is taken by a young man of large fortune from the north of England.
